questions.question: A "True Aneurysm" is morphologically defined as an enlargement of the vessel lumen to at least how many times its normal diameter?
questions.detail: A true aneurysm is defined as an enlargement of the lumen to 1.5 times its normal diameter.

questions.question: What is the defining anatomical characteristic of a "False (Pseudo) Aneurysm"?
choices: It involves all three layers of the vessel wall.
It is always fusiform in shape.
All three layers of the vessel wall are NOT intact.
It has a lower risk of rupture than a true aneurysm.
It is primarily caused by atherosclerosis.
questions.detail: In a false (pseudo) aneurysm, all three layers are NOT intact; it is essentially a contained rupture.

questions.question: On a pulsed Doppler ultrasound of a groin mass following a cardiac catheterization, "To and Fro" flow within a narrow neck is pathognomonic for:
questions.detail: To and Fro flow within the aneurysm neck is the best way to identify a pseudo-aneurysm.

questions.question: Where is the most common site to see a "Traumatic Pseudo-aneurysm" in a living patient who has survived a blunt injury?
questions.detail: The most common site for a traumatic pseudo-aneurysm in a living patient is the aortic isthmus (90%).

questions.question: Traumatic injury at the aortic isthmus is typically attributed to tethering by which structure?
questions.detail: The injury at the isthmus is the result of tethering from the ligamentum arteriosum.

questions.question: SVC Syndrome is most commonly caused by external compression from which of the following?
questions.detail: SVC syndrome occurs secondary to external compression (lymphoma, lung cancer) or intravascular obstruction.

questions.question: In the United States, "Fibrosing Mediastinitis" leading to SVC syndrome is most characteristically associated with which infection?
questions.detail: For fibrosing mediastinitis on a test, you should just think histoplasmosis.

questions.question: Sinus of Valsalva aneurysms are most common in which demographic?
questions.detail: These rare aneurysms are more common in Asian Men.

questions.question: Which cardiac anomaly is most frequently associated with a Sinus of Valsalva aneurysm?
questions.detail: VSD is the most common associated cardiac anomaly.

questions.question: On a non-contrast CT, the "Hyperdense Crescent Sign" within an aneurysm wall represents:
questions.detail: This sign represents hemorrhage and is a specific manifestation of impending rupture.

questions.question: The "Draped Aorta Sign" refers to the posterior wall of the aorta draping over the vertebral column and is an indicator of:
questions.detail: The draped aorta sign is a warning sign of impending rupture.

questions.question: If you see a saccular aneurysm of the abdominal aorta that lacks intimal calcifications and is expanding rapidly, which diagnosis must you lead with?
questions.detail: If you see a saccular aneurysm of the aorta (especially abdominal), you have to lead with infection.

questions.question: "Mycotic aneurysms" are most often saccular pseudo-aneurysms occurring via hematogenous seeding in the setting of:
questions.detail: They most often occur via seeding in the setting of septicemia (endocarditis).

questions.question: What are the two most common bacterial agents responsible for infectious aortitis?
questions.detail: The most common bugs are Staph A. and Salmonella.

questions.question: "Annuloaortic ectasia" and a "tulip bulb" appearance of the ascending aorta are classic imaging descriptions for:
questions.detail: Dilation in Marfans is classically described as "Annuloaortic ectasia" with a "tulip bulb" appearance.

questions.question: What is a common valvular complication resulting from the aortic root dilation in Marfan Syndrome?
questions.detail: Dilatation of the aortic root in Marfans leads to aortic valve insufficiency.

questions.question: Which syndrome is described as the "really shitty version of Marfans" and features extremely tortuous vessels and a bifid uvula?
questions.detail: Loeys-Dietz Syndrome features a triad of hypertelorism, bifid uvula or cleft palate, and very tortuous vessels.

questions.question: In "Secondary Aortoenteric Fistula," which segment of the gastrointestinal tract is most commonly involved?
questions.detail: The 3rd and 4th portions of the duodenum are the most common sites.

questions.question: Secondary aortoenteric fistulas are much more common than primary ones and are typically seen after:
choices: Surgery with or without stent graft placement
questions.detail: They are seen after surgery with or without stent graft placement.

questions.question: "Inflammatory Aneurysms" characteristically involve which demographic and which specific imaging finding?
choices: Elderly women; total aortic wall thickening
Young men who smoke; sparing of the posterior wall
questions.detail: These are seen in smokers and feature asymmetrical thickening with sparing of the posterior wall.

questions.question: "Leriche Syndrome" is characterized by a clinical triad of claudication, absent/decreased femoral pulses, and:
questions.detail: The Leriche triad includes impotence, ass claudication, and absence of femoral pulses.

questions.question: On a chest X-ray, the "Figure 3 Sign" is the classic "Aunt Minnie" for:
questions.detail: The Figure 3 sign is pathognomonic for coarctation.

questions.question: Rib notching in aortic coarctation typically involves the 4th through 8th ribs but characteristically spares which ribs?
choices: All ribs on the left side
questions.detail: Rib notching spares the 1st and 2nd ribs because they are fed by the costocervical trunk.

questions.question: "Pseudocoarctation" is distinguished from true coarctation by the ABSENCE of:
choices: A pressure gradient, collateral formation, and rib notching
questions.detail: In pseudocoarctation, there is NO pressure gradient, collateral formation, or rib notching.

questions.question: "Paget Schroetter" syndrome, also known as "effort thrombosis," involves a venous thrombus in which vessel?
questions.detail: It involves a venous thrombus in the Subclavian vein, often seen in athletes.

questions.question: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome is most commonly caused by neurogenic compression from which structure?
questions.detail: It is typically caused by compression by the anterior scalene muscle.

questions.question: "Cystic Medial Necrosis" is a pathological term most strongly associated with which condition?
questions.detail: When you say cystic medial necrosis, you should think Marfans.

questions.question: What is the surgical repair threshold for an asymptomatic aortic aneurysm in the chest?
questions.detail: A general rule is surgical repair for chest aneurysms at 6 cm (5.5 cm with collagen vascular disease).

questions.question: What are the two primary causes of ascending aortic calcifications?
questions.detail: Ascending aorta calcifications should make you say Syphilis and Takayasu.

questions.question: Which condition is associated with a "tree bark" appearance of the intimal calcifications?
questions.detail: Tree bark intimal calcification is the buzzword for a Syphilitic (Luetic) aneurysm.

questions.question: "Diverticulum of Kommerell" refers to an aneurysmal dilatation at the origin of which vessel?
questions.detail: It is an aneurysmal dilatation at the proximal portion of an aberrant right subclavian artery.

questions.question: Which vascular ring anomaly is the most common cause of symptoms like tracheal compression?
choices: Left arch with aberrant right subclavian
questions.detail: Double Aortic Arch is the most common symptomatic vascular ring.

questions.question: Symptoms of a Double Aortic Arch typically manifest at what age?
questions.detail: Symptoms of this ring may begin at birth.

questions.question: A "Pulmonary Sling" is uniquely associated with which other primary pathology?
questions.detail: It is associated with tracheal stenosis from complete rings.

questions.question: Vascular findings in Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF-1) include which renal presentation in children?
choices: Orificial renal artery stenosis and hypertension
questions.detail: The classic look is orificial renal artery stenosis presenting with hypertension in a child.

questions.question: "Chronic" Mesenteric Ischemia is typically diagnosed when there is significant stenosis of how many main vessels?
questions.detail: Chronic ischemia usually involves stenosis of 2 out of 3 main mesenteric vessels.

questions.question: What is the classic clinical hallmark of Chronic Mesenteric Ischemia?
choices: "Food fear" and pain out of proportion to exam
questions.detail: Hallmarks include "food fear" and pain out of proportion to physical findings.

questions.question: Which location is the most common site for mesenteric ischemia because it is the watershed of the SMA and IMA?
questions.detail: The splenic flexure ("Griffith's Point") is the most common site.

questions.question: "Sudeck’s Point" is another watershed area highly susceptible to ischemia located at the:
questions.detail: Sudeck’s Point is the IMA-Iliac watershed.

questions.question: "Acute" Mesenteric Ischemia is most commonly (~70%) caused by which of the following?
questions.detail: Arterial causes (emboli or thrombus) account for ~70% of acute cases.

questions.question: In the initial phase of acute arterial ischemia, the bowel wall characteristically appears:
choices: Thinner (no arterial inflow) and not typically dilated
questions.detail: Initially, the wall is thinner (no arterial inflow) and not typically dilated.

questions.question: Following reperfusion of an ischemic bowel segment, the bowel wall typically becomes:
questions.detail: After reperfusion, the bowel becomes thick with a target appearance.

questions.question: "Venous" Mesenteric Ischemia (~15%) is characterized by which imaging findings?
choices: Dilation with significant wall thickening (8-9 mm), fat stranding, and ascites
Air in the portal vein only
questions.detail: Venous occlusion causes dilation, wall thickening (8-9 mm), fat stranding, and ascites.

questions.question: Non-Occlusive Mesenteric Ischemia (NOMI) is primarily caused by:
choices: A large clot in the SMA
questions.detail: NOMI is caused by systemic hypoperfusion.

questions.question: Which "sneaky scenario" can lead to NOMI due to mesenteric vasospasm?
questions.detail: Amphetamines or cocaine can trigger the vasospasm.

questions.question: A clue to diagnosing NOMI on a CT scan is:
choices: Delayed filling of the portal vein (not filled by 70 seconds)
Reflux of contrast into the hepatic veins
questions.detail: Delayed filling of the portal vein is a clue.

questions.question: "Strangulation" ischemia is almost always secondary to:
questions.detail: This is almost always secondary to a closed loop obstruction.

questions.question: Mesenteric ischemia has a recognized association with which arterial abnormality?
questions.detail: Mesenteric ischemia has a described association with SMA Aneurysm.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of portal hypertension in North America?
questions.detail: In North America, the most common cause is EtOH (alcoholic cirrhosis).

questions.question: Portal hypertension is defined by a portal vein pressure greater than:
questions.detail: Portal HTN is defined as pressure > 10 mm Hg (or PSG > 5 mm Hg).

questions.question: On ultrasound, an enlarged portal vein measurement suggesting hypertension is:
questions.detail: An enlarged portal vein (>1.3-1.5 cm) is a sign of portal HTN.

questions.question: Most gastric varices are formed by which vessel?
questions.detail: Most gastric varices are formed by the left gastric (coronary) vein.

questions.question: "Isolated" gastric varices should prompt a search for:
questions.detail: Isolated gastric varices are secondary to splenic vein thrombosis (often due to pancreatitis).

questions.question: A "Splenorenal Shunt" is a collateral pathway in portal hypertension described as:
choices: Highly dangerous and prone to bleeding
Desirable because it is NOT associated with GI bleeding
questions.detail: It is a desirable collateral because it is NOT associated with GI bleeding.

questions.question: What is the goal portal-systemic gradient (PSG) after a successful TIPS procedure?
questions.detail: The GOAL for a successful TIPS is a gradient LESS THAN 12.

questions.question: Which of the following is an ABSOLUTE contraindication for a TIPS procedure?
questions.detail: Heart failure (especially right heart failure) is an absolute contraindication.

questions.question: "BRTO" (Balloon-occluded Retrograde Transvenous Obliteration) is used to treat:
choices: Gastric varices (in the presence of a gastro-renal shunt)
questions.detail: BRTO treats gastric varices and requires a gastro-renal shunt.

questions.question: What is the most common side effect following a BRTO procedure?
questions.detail: The most common side effect is gross hematuria.

questions.question: "Budd-Chiari Syndrome" results from:
choices: Aortic dissection involving the celiac axis
Celiac artery compression by a ligament
questions.detail: This results from thrombosis of the hepatic veins.

questions.question: The characteristic imaging finding of acute Budd-Chiari is a "flip-flop pattern" where the liver shows:
choices: Central enhancement with low peripheral attenuation on portal phase
questions.detail: Acute Budd-Chiari shows low attenuation centrally and high attenuation peripherally on portal phase.

questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with renovascular hypertension. CT shows a "string of beads" appearance of the renal arteries. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: FMD in young women classically presents as "string of beads".

questions.question: FMD characteristically involves which segment of the renal artery?
questions.detail: FMD favors the distal artery, whereas atherosclerosis favors the proximal and NF-1 favors the ostium.

questions.question: Which vascular finding is specifically associated with FMD?
questions.detail: SCAD is associated with FMD.

questions.question: "Nutcracker Syndrome" occurs when the left renal vein is compressed between which two structures?
questions.detail: The left renal vein is smashed between the SMA and the aorta.

questions.question: Clinical symptoms of Nutcracker Syndrome include left flank pain, hematuria, and:
choices: Left testicle pain in men or LLQ pain in women
questions.detail: It can cause left testicle pain ("nut cracker") or LLQ pain in women.

questions.question: What is the gold standard for diagnosing Nutcracker Syndrome?
choices: Retrograde venography with renal/caval pressure measurements
questions.detail: The gold standard for Nutcracker is retrograde venography with pressure measurements.

questions.question: "Median Arcuate Ligament Syndrome" (Dunbar Syndrome) involves compression of which vessel?
questions.detail: This syndrome involves compression of the celiac artery by the median arcuate ligament.

questions.question: The characteristic radiographic buzzword for Median Arcuate Ligament Syndrome is:
questions.detail: A "hooked appearance" is the characteristic buzzword for this compression.

questions.question: Compression in Median Arcuate Ligament Syndrome characteristically gets WORSE during:
questions.detail: The compression classically gets worse with expiration.

questions.question: "Pelvic Congestion Syndrome" is typically seen in which demographic?
choices: Depressed, multiparous, pre-menopausal women with chronic pelvic pain
questions.detail: It is seen in pre-menopausal women with chronic pelvic pain and dilated parauterine veins.

questions.question: "May-Thurner Syndrome" results in DVT of the left common iliac vein due to compression by:
questions.detail: The left common iliac vein is compressed by the right common iliac artery.

questions.question: A patient with May-Thurner Syndrome will typically present with:
questions.detail: They present with a swollen left leg.

questions.question: "Popliteal Aneurysm" is the most common peripheral arterial aneurysm and has a strong association with:
questions.detail: There is a strong and frequent association with AAA (30-50% have one).

questions.question: What is the most dreaded complication of a popliteal artery aneurysm?
questions.detail: The most dreaded complication is distal emboli, which can be limb-threatening.

questions.question: A "Popliteal Entrapment" syndrome in a young man (<30) is caused by the medial deviation of the artery by the:
questions.detail: It is caused by the medial head of the gastrocnemius.

questions.question: "Cystic Adventitial Disease" classically affects the popliteal artery of young men and is characterized by:
choices: Multiple mucoid-filled cysts in the vessel wall that compress the lumen
questions.detail: It features mucoid-filled cysts that compress the artery.

questions.question: "Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome" results from blunt trauma to the:
questions.detail: It results from trauma to the ulnar artery (e.g., using the palm as a hammer).

questions.question: The angiographic hallmark of Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome is:
choices: Corkscrew configuration or pseudo-aneurysm of the ulnar artery
questions.detail: Look for a "corkscrew" appearance or pseudo-aneurysm of the ulnar artery.

questions.question: What is the classic "next step" if you see an isolated right-sided varicocele in an adult?
choices: Get an abdominal CT/MR to look for a mass/malignancy
questions.detail: An isolated right varicocele is a red flag to look in the belly for badness (like RCC).

questions.question: "Klippel-Trenaunay Syndrome" (KTS) features a triad of port wine nevi, soft tissue/bony hypertrophy, and which vascular finding?
questions.detail: The marginal vein of Servelle (a large saphenous on the wrong side) is pathognomonic for KTS.

questions.question: "Osler-Weber-Rendu" (HHT) is characterized by multiple AVMs in which two primary organs?
questions.detail: HHT features multiple AVMs in the liver and lungs.

questions.question: What is the primary cause of death in patients with HHT due to pulmonary AVMs?
choices: Stroke or brain abscess (via paradoxical emboli)
questions.detail: Most die from stroke or brain abscess.

questions.question: At what afferent vessel size is a pulmonary AVM typically treated with coil embolization?
questions.detail: The rule is to treat once the afferent (feeding) artery is 3 mm.

questions.question: "Colonic Angiodysplasia" is primarily right-sided and associated with which valvular condition (Heyde Syndrome)?
questions.detail: The association with aortic stenosis is called Heyde Syndrome.

questions.question: "Artery of Adamkiewicz" most commonly arises from the left side of the aorta between which levels?
questions.detail: It arises from the left side (70%) typically between levels T9 and T12 (90%).

questions.question: On an angiogram, the Artery of Adamkiewicz is identified by its characteristic:
questions.detail: The morphology on an angiogram is a "hairpin turn".

questions.question: "Nutcracker Syndrome" compression of the left renal vein can also cause pain in which location?
questions.detail: Nutcracker syndrome can cause left testicle pain or LLQ pain in women.

questions.question: "Uterine AVMs" are most commonly acquired following which event?
questions.detail: Acquired uterine AVMs occur after D&C, abortion, or multiple pregnancies.

questions.question: "Segmental Arterial Mediolysis" (SAM) targets the splanchnic arteries of the elderly and causes:
choices: Multiple abdominal splanchnic artery saccular aneurysms and dissections
questions.detail: Hallmarks include multiple saccular aneurysms, dissections, and occlusions in splanchnic arteries.

questions.question: What is the diagnostic size for a "Pelvic Congestion Syndrome" gonadal vein?
questions.detail: Diagnosis involves clinical symptoms and a gonadal vein diameter of 10 mm.

questions.question: "Buerger Disease" (Thromboangiitis Obliterans) is a vasculitis exclusively seen in:
questions.detail: Buerger disease is strongly and exclusively associated with smokers.

questions.question: The angiographic buzzword for Buerger Disease is:
questions.detail: Features include corkscrew collaterals and "Auto-amputation".

questions.question: "Takayasu Arteritis" is a large-vessel vasculitis that characteristically affects:
choices: Infants with a history of fever
questions.detail: Takayasu characteristically favors young Asian girls aged 15-30.

questions.question: "Giant Cell Arteritis" (GCA) classically involves the temporal artery and which other region?
questions.detail: GCA involves vessels in the temporal region and the armpit.

questions.question: "Polyarteritis Nodosa" (PAN) is a medium-vessel vasculitis with a strong association with:
questions.detail: PAN is strongly associated with Hepatitis B.

questions.question: "Kawasaki Disease" is a primary cause of which vascular complication in children?
questions.detail: Kawasaki is well-known for causing coronary vessel aneurysms in children.

questions.question: Coronary artery aneurysms in Kawasaki disease are considered "Giant" if they measure:
questions.detail: Aneurysms > 8 mm are "Giant" and prone to MI.

questions.question: "Behcet Disease" is a small-vessel vasculitis that is a known cause of:
questions.detail: Behcet Disease is high-yield for causing pulmonary artery aneurysms.

questions.question: A "Rasmussen Aneurysm" is a pulmonary artery pseudo-aneurysm secondary to:
questions.detail: This is a pseudo-aneurysm secondary to pulmonary TB.

questions.question: "Hughes-Stovin Syndrome" is characterized by recurrent thrombophlebitis and:
choices: Pulmonary artery aneurysm formation and rupture
questions.detail: This condition is characterized by pulmonary artery aneurysm formation and rupture.
